svg.title: Gray-Scott horizon drift tags
svg.desc: The same feed-vs-kill grid at 700, 1400, and 2800 steps. A bounded tag pass for which cells settled, grew, faded, or reversed.
svg.text: The same feed-vs-kill grid at 700, 1400, and 2800 steps. A bounded tag pass for which cells settled, grew, faded, or reversed.

text.tspan: Each cell reads the three-horizon active-fraction path only.
Descriptive tags: settled, late-growing, late-fading, or reversing.
This isolates the second leg only.
Positive means late fill-in. Negative means the middle horizon overread the cell.
Biggest late growth: F=0.022, k=0.054, Δactive=+0.139.
Biggest late fade: F=0.026, k=0.051, Δactive=-1.000.
Read the left panel as the bounded classifier and the right panel as the raw second-leg drift.
Together they show that the unsettled cells do not all fail the same way: some keep growing, some fade out, and a smaller set overshoot and then reverse.
F=0.030, k=0.060 stays within ±0.016 active-fraction drift while edge density only moves by 0.0039 per leg.
F=0.030, k=0.063 finishes +0.130 higher in active fraction by the late horizon with a last-leg edge change of +0.0096.
F=0.026, k=0.051 loses 1.000 active fraction by the late horizon and ends near 0.000 occupancy.
F=0.022, k=0.051 changes sign between legs: +0.321 first, then -0.174.
This is still a bounded read built from one lattice, one seed, and one active-fraction rule.
That is enough to sharpen the repo’s caveat story without pretending the whole Gray-Scott plane has been universally classified.
